---
title: "R for Health Data Science"
author: 
  - "Heather Waddell"
  - "Alexandra Richards"
  - "Clarisse de Vries"
  - "Jocelyn Friday"
  - "Frederick Ho"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
documentclass: book
bibliography: [_references.bib, _packages.bib]
biblio-style: agsm
cover-image: images/W0-GlaLogo.png
description: |
  This is a companion ebook for the MED5686 - R for Health Data Science course 
  in School of Health and Wellbeing, University of Glasgow.
link-citations: yes
github-repo: rstudio/bookdown-demo
---

# About This Ebook {-}

![](images/00-GlaLogo.png){width="300px"}

This is a companion ebook for the MED5686 - R for Health Data Science course in School of Health and Wellbeing, University of Glasgow.

## How to Use This Ebook {-}

::: yellowbox
A box in this colour will often come in two sections: the top will be example
code that you should be able to copy into your own R programme and run, and
under the dashed line, the output of that code.
:::

::: pinkbox
A box in this colour will include suggestions for things to try to help improve
your understanding of the previous section.
:::

The following text formations have been used throughout the document to help
with understanding:

-   Bolded words, such as **this**, either refer to a specific R package, which
    will have an associated glossary entry, or the word(s) will refer to a
    specific concept.
-   Monospace font words, such as `this`, indicate code. These words may or may
    not be black.\
-   Italicised words, such as *this*, indicate datasets that will be used
    throughout this course.

## Data Sets {- #sec:dataSets} 

This document was built with the intention of acting as an introduction to R. To
do that, it draws upon data provided and modified from the following sources:

-   The '*02-hrqol_data.csv*' is a provided, simulated dataset used in 
    Chapters \@ref(ch-2) to practice importing into R.
-   The '*who*'[^index-1] dataset is an inbuilt dataset for the list of
    countries and is used in Chapter \@ref(ch-3).
-   The '*03-diabetic_demog.csv*' dataset is a provided dataset used in Chapter
    \@ref(ch-3) and \@ref(ch-5) for merging.
-   The '*03-diabetic_labs_final_with_missing.csv*' dataset is a provided dataset 
    used in Chapters \@ref(ch-3) and \@ref(ch-5) for missing data and merging.
-   The '*iris*' dataset is an inbuilt dataset for simple classifications and
    used in Chapter \@ref(ch-4) for data visualisation.
-   The '*06-alert_rct.dta*' dataset is a provided dataset on a real-world 
    randomised controlled trial data [@holdaas2003effect] and used 
    in \@ref(ch-6) for survival analysis
-   The '*06-understanding_soc.csv*' dataset is a provided dataset on a 
    observational, prospective cohort study [@buck2012understanding] used in 
    Chapters \@ref(ch-6) and \@ref(ch-7) for various advanced topic in 
    regression analysis, and causal inference.
-   The '*07-iv.csv*' dataset is a provided dataset on a simulated intervention 
    to practice instrumental variable analyss in  Chapter \@ref(ch-7).

[^index-1]: The capitalisation here is to match the name of the dataset within
    the survival package.

The *03-diabetic_demog.csv* and *03-diabetic_labs_final_with_missing.csv* were 
created by combining information from the diabetic dataset in the survival package 
and the 'The Health Test by Blood Dataset' [@kaggleDM] for predicting the 
development of \acr{DM}. The variables for *diabeticDemog* are described in 
Table \@ref(tab:diabeticDemog) and the variables for *diabeticLabs* are described 
in Table \@ref(tab:diabeticLabs). The provided datasets are available on the 
course Moodle page.

```{r diabeticDemog, echo=FALSE}
tab_demog <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("id", "age", "Gender", "BMI", "SIMD"),
  Description = c("Patient id", "Age at diagnosis", "Patient's gender", "Patient BMI at baseline", "Patient SIMD quintile at baseline")
)
knitr::kable(tab_demog, caption = "A description of the variables available within the diabeticDemog dataset.")
```

It is important to note that sex and gender are not the same. In electronic
patient records, biological sex is usually recorded and used for analysis. In
this case, gender was used, since this is what @kaggleDM recorded in the 'The
Health Test by Blood' dataset. \acr{SIMD} was added to the diabeticDemog dataset
for some local flair. \acr{SIMD} is an area-based measurement of socioeconomic
deprivation assigned to residents of Scotland according to where they live.
Scottish residents' \acr{SIMD} status is calculated by the Scottish Government
using thirty-one indicators from seven different aspects of deprivation: income,
employment, health, education, housing, geographic access, and crime. The
indicators are combined using a weighted sum to create a single index, providing
a relative ranking for each small geographic area in Scotland. The areas average
approximately 800 individuals [@Executive2012]. It is important to note that
\acr{SIMD} can only measure the level of deprivation in an area, not an
individual's level. The absence of deprivation should not necessarily be
correlated with affluence [@Executive2012].

```{r diabeticLabs, echo=FALSE}
tab_labs <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("id", "SampleDate", "SampleTime", "Chol", "TG", "HDL", "LDL", "Cr", "BUN"),
  Description = c("Patient id", "The date the blood sample was taken", "The time on a 24-hour scale the blood sample was taken", "Cholesterol (mmol/L)", "Triglycerides (mmol/L)", "HDL (mmol/L)", "LDL (mmol/L)", "Serum creatinine (µmol/L)", "Blood urea nitrogen (mmol/L)")
)
knitr::kable(tab_labs, caption = "A description of the variables available within the diabeticLabs dataset.")
```
